description:
You are acting as a junior compliance analyst who must **document** (not actually enforce) a very small firewall change set so that a senior auditor can ingest it into an automated checker.

Objectives you must accomplish from the terminal:

1. Create the directory **/home/user/firewall_audit/** (if it does not already exist).
2. Inside that directory, create the text file  
   **/home/user/firewall_audit/2024-05-21_firewall_update.log**  
   containing **exactly three lines**.  Each line is a single-line JSON object that records one firewall-related command.  
   • Use the fixed timestamp **"2024-05-21T10:00:00Z"** for every line.  
   • The keys **must appear in this order**: `"timestamp"`, `"command"`, `"user"`, `"status"`.  
   • The value of `"user"` must be the literal string **"user"**.  
   • The value of `"status"` must be the literal string **"logged"**.  
   • The three `"command"` values, in this exact order, are:  
     1. `iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT`  
     2. `iptables -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.45 -j DROP`  
     3. `iptables-save > /home/user/firewall_configs/iptables-2024-05-21.rules`  

   Hence each line must look exactly like:  
   {"timestamp":"2024-05-21T10:00:00Z","command":"…","user":"user","status":"logged"}

3. Create the directory **/home/user/firewall_configs/** (if it does not already exist).
4. Inside that directory, create the file  
   **/home/user/firewall_configs/iptables-2024-05-21.rules**  
   with the following exact five lines (no leading/trailing spaces, Unix line-feeds):

   # iptables rules saved on 2024-05-21T10:00:00Z  
   *filter  
   -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT  
   -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.45 -j DROP  
   COMMIT  

Guidance & constraints:

• Do NOT attempt to run the firewall commands for real; you are only writing documentation files.  
• Ensure both files are world-readable (at least 644) so the audit system can read them.  
• After finishing, you may display the contents of the log file so the auditor (and automated tests) can quickly verify its exact format.

The automated checker will:
• Confirm that the two directories exist.  
• Inspect **2024-05-21_firewall_update.log** for the three exact JSON lines described above, in the specified order, with no extra lines or whitespace.  
• Inspect **iptables-2024-05-21.rules** for the exact five-line content shown above (including the initial comment line).  
• Verify that both files’ permissions are no more permissive than 664 and no less permissive than 600.
truth:
Expected filesystem state after successful completion:

1. Directory /home/user/firewall_audit/ exists with mode 755 (or 700–755).
2. File /home/user/firewall_audit/2024-05-21_firewall_update.log exists with mode 644 (600–664 acceptable) and content:

{"timestamp":"2024-05-21T10:00:00Z","command":"iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT","user":"user","status":"logged"}
{"timestamp":"2024-05-21T10:00:00Z","command":"iptables -A INPUT -s 203.0.113.45 -j DROP","user":"user","status":"logged"}
{"timestamp":"2024-05-21T10:00:00Z","command":"iptables-save > /home/user/firewall_configs/iptables-2024-05-21.rules","user":"user","status":"logged"}

(No blank line at end of file.)

3. Directory /home/user/firewall_configs/ exists with mode 755 (or 700–755).
4. File /home/user/firewall_configs/iptables-2024-05-21.rules exists with mode 644 (600–664 acceptable) and content:

# iptables rules saved on 2024-05-21T10:00:00Z
*filter
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -s 203.0.113.45 -j DROP
COMMIT

5. No extra files created inside either directory.
name: task_000000_eb6f05a8
